title: Coxsone Dodd

Clement Seymour Dodd  (1932-01-26)26 January 1932  [Kingston](/wiki/Kingston,_Jamaica "Kingston, Jamaica"), Jamaica

[newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Coxsone+Dodd%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Coxsone+Dodd%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Coxsone+Dodd%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Coxsone+Dodd%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(November 2009)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

"**Coxsone**" **Dodd** [CD](/wiki/Order_of_Distinction_(Jamaica) "Order of Distinction (Jamaica)") (26 January 1932 – 4 May 2004) was a Jamaican record producer who was influential in the development of [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") and [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae") in the 1950s, 1960s and beyond.

When journalist Roger Steffens asked Dodd about the spelling of his name, he asserted that it was "Coxson" without the 'e' found on the spelling of the record label "Coxsone". Steffens referenced this anecdote in *The Beat* magazine and *The Reggae Scrapbook* (Insight Editions, San Rafael, CA, 2007), p. 33.
Katz, David. ["Obituary: Clement 'Sir Coxsone' Dodd"](https://www.theguardian.com/news/2004/may/06/guardianobituaries.artsobituaries), TheGuardian.com, 5 May 2004; retrieved 3 December 2016.
["Andy & Joey – You're Wondering Now / You'll Never"](https://www.discogs.com/Andy-Joey-Youre-Wondering-Now-Youll-Never/release/4519549). *Discogs.com*. Zink Media, Inc. Retrieved 23 February 2018.

Musical artist

**Clement Seymour** "**Coxsone**" **Dodd** [CD](/wiki/Order_of_Distinction_(Jamaica) "Order of Distinction (Jamaica)") (26 January 1932 – 4 May 2004) was a Jamaican record producer who was influential in the development of [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") and [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae") in the 1950s, 1960s and beyond.

He was nicknamed "Coxsone" at school due to his talent as a [cricketer](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") (his friends compared him to [Alec Coxon](/wiki/Alec_Coxon "Alec Coxon"), a member of the 1940s [Yorkshire County Cricket Club](/wiki/Yorkshire_County_Cricket_Club "Yorkshire County Cricket Club") team).[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coxsone_Dodd&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

The [Kingston](/wiki/Kingston,_Jamaica "Kingston, Jamaica")-born Dodd used to play records to the customers in his parents' shop. During a spell in the [American South](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States") he became familiar with the [rhythm and blues](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues") music popular there at the time. In 1954, back in Jamaica, he set up the Downbeat Sound System, being the owner of an [amplifier](/wiki/Amplifier "Amplifier"), a [turntable](/wiki/Phonograph "Phonograph"), and some US records, which he would import from [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans,_Louisiana "New Orleans, Louisiana") and [Miami](/wiki/Miami,_Florida "Miami, Florida").

With the success of his [sound system](/wiki/Sound_system_(Jamaican) "Sound system (Jamaican)"), and in a competitive environment, Dodd would make trips through the US looking for new tunes to attract the Jamaican public.[[2]](#cite_note-Larkin-2) While he did, his mother [Doris Darlington](/wiki/Doris_Darlington "Doris Darlington") would run the sound system and play the tunes. Dodd opened five different sound systems, each playing every night. To run his sound systems, Dodd appointed people such as [Lee "Scratch" Perry](/wiki/Lee_%22Scratch%22_Perry "Lee \"Scratch\" Perry"), who was Dodd's right-hand man during his early career, [U-Roy](/wiki/U-Roy "U-Roy") and [Prince Buster](/wiki/Prince_Buster "Prince Buster").[[3]](#cite_note-3) Perry would later leave Dodd in 1966 due to Perry feeling disrespected by Dodd. This is documented in the 1966 song "[The Upsetter](/w/index.php?title=The_Upsetter_(song)&action=edit&redlink=1 "The Upsetter (song) (page does not exist)")".

### Recording career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coxsone_Dodd&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Recording career")]

When the R&B craze ended in the United States, Dodd and his rivals were forced to begin recording their own Jamaican music in order to meet the local demand for new music.[[2]](#cite_note-Larkin-2)

In 1959, he founded a record company called Worldisc. In 1962, he produced the [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") record "[I Cover the Waterfront](/wiki/I_Cover_the_Waterfront_(song) "I Cover the Waterfront (song)")" on the [Port-O-Jam](/w/index.php?title=Port-O-Jam&action=edit&redlink=1 "Port-O-Jam (page does not exist)") label; two of the musicians who played on the album, [Roland Alphonso](/wiki/Roland_Alphonso "Roland Alphonso") and [Don Drummond](/wiki/Don_Drummond "Don Drummond"), became founding members of [the Skatalites](/wiki/The_Skatalites "The Skatalites") one year later.[[2]](#cite_note-Larkin-2) In 1963, he opened [Studio One](/wiki/Studio_One_(record_label) "Studio One (record label)") on Brentford Road, Kingston.[[2]](#cite_note-Larkin-2)[[4]](#cite_note-nytobit-4) It was the first black-owned recording studio in Jamaica. He held regular Sunday evening auditions in search of new talent, and it was here that Dodd auditioned [Bob Marley](/wiki/Bob_Marley "Bob Marley"), singing as a part of [the Wailers](/wiki/The_Wailers "The Wailers").

In the early 1960s, Dodd was producing [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") hits by [Toots and the Maytals](/wiki/Toots_and_the_Maytals "Toots and the Maytals"), [the Gaylads](/wiki/The_Gaylads "The Gaylads"), and [the Skatalites](/wiki/The_Skatalites "The Skatalites").[[5]](#cite_note-Guardianobit-5)

Dodd's "You're Wondering Now", was initially recorded in 1964 by Andy & Joey in Jamaica[[6]](#cite_note-Discogs_Andy_&_Joey-6) and later covered by [the Skatalites](/wiki/The_Skatalites "The Skatalites"), [the Specials](/wiki/The_Specials_(album)#Track_listing "The Specials (album)") and [Amy Winehouse](/wiki/List_of_songs_recorded_by_Amy_Winehouse "List of songs recorded by Amy Winehouse"); it was also used as the theme tune for the British-French crime drama television series *[Death in Paradise](/wiki/Death_in_Paradise "Death in Paradise")*.

During the late 1960s and 1970s, the "Studio One sound" was synonymous with the sound of [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska"), [rocksteady](/wiki/Rocksteady "Rocksteady") and [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae"), and Dodd attracted some of the Jamaican new musicians, including [Burning Spear](/wiki/Burning_Spear "Burning Spear"), [Ras Michael](/wiki/Ras_Michael "Ras Michael"), [Delroy Wilson](/wiki/Delroy_Wilson "Delroy Wilson"), [Horace Andy](/wiki/Horace_Andy "Horace Andy"), Sound Dimension, and [Sugar Minott](/wiki/Sugar_Minott "Sugar Minott").[[2]](#cite_note-Larkin-2)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In the mid-1980s, Dodd closed his studio and moved his base of operations to [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City").[[8]](#cite_note-LarkinReggae-8)

## Honours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coxsone_Dodd&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Honours")]

In 2002 he was awarded a Gold [Musgrave Medal](/wiki/Musgrave_Medal "Musgrave Medal") by the [Institute of Jamaica](/wiki/Institute_of_Jamaica "Institute of Jamaica").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Last years and death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coxsone_Dodd&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Last years and death")]

He continued to be active in the [music industry](/wiki/Music_industry "Music industry") into his seventies, and on 1 May 2004, Kingston's Brentford Road was renamed Studio One Boulevard in a ceremony which paid tribute to his accomplishments as a producer. He died suddenly of a [heart attack](/wiki/Myocardial_infarction "Myocardial infarction") three days later, aged 72, while working at Studio One.[[4]](#cite_note-nytobit-4) He was survived by his wife, Norma, who died in 2010.[[10]](#cite_note-jamaicaobserver-10)

Dodd was posthumously awarded the [Order of Distinction](/wiki/Order_of_Distinction_(Jamaica) "Order of Distinction (Jamaica)"), in the rank of Commander on 15 October 2007, for service to the Jamaica music industry.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coxsone_Dodd&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: See also")]
